content.content:
# auxilio-extension

Google **Chrome extension (MV3)** for Auxilio (Office Ops) — the **desktop** surface for registering office visitors directly inside Google Calendar. It is the richer counterpart to the `auxilio-workspace` Calendar add-on: where the add-on is mobile-capable but lives under the CardService "card ceiling," the extension gives a full React UI (realtime guest updates, per-guest invite toggle, template preview, inline edits, intentional auto-nudge).

> Read the umbrella `../CLAUDE.md` for the four constraints and UX rules — they apply here.
> Strategy + approval analysis: `../Planning-docs/2026-06-18_calendar_visitor_chrome_extension_decision.md`.
> Build plan: the approved plan that scaffolded this repo.

## What this is (and isn't)

- **Is:** an MV3 extension (WXT + React 19 + TS) that injects a trigger into Google Calendar (web) and renders the registration flow in the **side panel**. A **channel surface / dumb adapter** — it forwards intent to `office-ops-engine`.
- **Isn't:** a place for business logic. Dedup, draft curation, invite/pass issuance, and lifecycle all live in `office-ops-engine`'s visitor plugin. This repo renders UI and calls the engine.
- **Isn't:** mobile. Extensions are desktop-Chrome only. The `auxilio-workspace` add-on remains the mobile surface. Both key off the same **magic address**.

## Architecture — three MV3 surfaces

- **`entrypoints/background.ts`** (service worker) — the only holder of tokens + network. Mints/caches the Google `id_token` (engine auth) + `access_token` (Calendar read), runs the Calendar API client and the engine `/addon/*` client, keeps per-tab event context, routes messages.
- **`entrypoints/calendar.content.ts`** (content script @ `calendar.google.com`) — a **dumb trigger/observer**: detect open event, decode `eid`, inject the "Register a visitor" button (Phase 1) and the magic-address banner (Phase 2). No tokens, no business logic.
- **`entrypoints/sidepanel/`** (the app) — React + TanStack Query UI. Pure presentation + server state.

```
content script ──msg──> background ──HTTPS(id_token)──> office-ops-engine /api/visitor/calendar/addon/*
side panel    ──msg──> background ──Calendar API(access_token)──> canonical iCalUID + attendees
```

## Hard rules

1. **Calendar-only scopes. Never add Gmail/Drive.** Calendar is *sensitive* (verification, no paid audit). Gmail/Drive are *restricted* → annual CASA assessment. Hard line, same as the add-on.
2. **Engine owns the contract.** The extension calls the existing `/api/visitor/calendar/addon/{draft,preview,send,cancel-guest}` routes (ID-token authed). Do **not** add engine signature/scope changes from here. The only engine-side change is env: add this extension's OAuth client id to `AUXILIO_WORKSPACE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` (audience allow-list).
3. **Background is the only secret/network holder.** Content script stays a dumb trigger; side panel stays presentation. Keep the thin-adapter boundary.
4. **Send the canonical `iCalUID`** (from the Calendar API `events.get`, not synthesized) so the extension path converges on the same `calendar_event_links` row as `.ics`/OAuth/add-on.
5. **Design: Material 3 tokens, not its code.** Our own `design/tokens.ts` + `global.css` (primary `#92288E`). No MUI/Tailwind/paper. **Icons: lucide-react only**, no Unicode glyphs/emoji in chrome.
6. **Animations subtle, only where needed** — CSS transitions driven by motion tokens; respect `prefers-reduced-motion`.
7. **Internal-first.** Force-installed/unlisted to our Workspace → no OAuth verification yet. Public/commercial = a later packaging + verification exercise (CWS review + OAuth verification), per the decision doc.

## Stack & commands

WXT (MV3 over Vite) + React 19 + TypeScript + TanStack Query (server state) + Zustand (UI/session/event-context) + lucide-react.

```bash
npm install
npm run dev        # WXT dev server; load .output/chrome-mv3 in chrome://extensions (Developer mode → Load unpacked)
npm run build      # production build → .output/chrome-mv3
npm run compile    # tsc --noEmit (type-check)
npm run zip        # packaged zip for store upload
```

Env: copy `.env.example` → `.env` (engine URL, OAuth client id, magic address). `WXT_` vars are inlined at build.

## Deploy / distribution

**Not Bifrost, not EAS.** Internal-first via Chrome admin **force-install** (unlisted, by extension id) or "Load unpacked" for dev. Public later via the Chrome Web Store (payments handled by our own engine; CWS no longer bills).

content.text:
Both type-check and build pass. Here's what I changed and why, framed against our MD3-tokens stance.

## What was wrong
The per-guest "card" (`RosterRow`) was really a flat list row: a bare switch on the left, a name string, then plain stacked inputs that appeared below. With `.row { align-items: center }`, the switch floated to the vertical middle of the whole row once the inputs expanded — so the control drifted away from the thing it controlled. There was no identity anchor, no contained grouping for the edit fields, and toggling "off" silently did nothing visible, so the switch's meaning wasn't obvious.

## What I did (all on our own tokens — no framework added)

**`RosterRow.tsx` — proper MD3 list item.**
- **Avatar monogram** (e.g. "JD" from name, or the email local-part) anchors each guest visually and recolors by state: primary-container normally, success when a pass is sent, muted when excluded.
- Identity (name + chips + email) sits as the headline/supporting text; the **switch is now trailing and vertically centered against the identity block** — it stays put whether the row is collapsed or expanded.
- The edit fields live in a **recessed `surface-low` container indented under the name**, so they read as "this guest's details," with a subtle expand animation (respecting `prefers-reduced-motion` via the existing global rule).
- **Honest feedback for "off":** an excluded guest now shows *"Won't receive a pass — toggle on to invite"* instead of just dimming silently, so the toggle teaches its own meaning.

**`Switch.tsx` — MD3's signature checkmark.** The thumb now carries a check glyph that fades in when on. State reads at a glance instead of relying on color/position alone.

**`TextField.tsx` — leading-icon support.** New optional `leadingIcon` prop (Lucide only). The name field gets a `User` icon, the phone field a `Phone` icon; the icon picks up the primary color on focus.

**`global.css`** — added `.guest*` styles, `.field__icon`/`--with-icon`, and `.switch__check`; kept `.row*` intact since `EmptyState` and others still use those.

A couple of judgment calls worth surfacing: I kept the switch size at 44×24 (this is a desktop, mouse-driven Chrome surface, not the low-end Android baseline), and I added the leading-icon capability to the shared `TextField` rather than one-off styling, since it's reusable across the extension. If you'd rather the edit form not be indented (some prefer full-width on a narrow side panel), that's a one-line change.

I haven't committed anything, per repo rules. Want me to load the unpacked build and screenshot it, or adjust the visual direction first?

content.text:
Exact match. To summarize:

## Answer: No, Google didn't change the URL

Both eids decode to the **same event id** (`786oqbqr7623gebqn84efa059g`). The only difference is the calendar-id segment that's encoded alongside it:

| | calendar-id segment |
|---|---|
| Google's real editor URL | `junaid@appointy.com` (your primary calendar's id) |
| What "Manage" was opening | `primary` (literal string) |

Google's `/r/eventedit/<eid>` expects the eid to be `base64("<eventId> <realCalendarId>")`, and for the primary calendar the real id is your email — not the word `"primary"`. The Calendar **API** happens to accept `"primary"` as an alias, which is why resolution/data still worked, but the **web editor URL** can't resolve it, so "Manage" landed on a broken/blank edit page.

## Fix
Our sync/nudge events were encoded as `encodeEid(ev.id, 'primary')` (`lib/calendar-sync.ts:54`), and `NAVIGATE_TO_EVENT` used that eid verbatim. I added a step in the navigation handler (`background.ts`) that, just before building the URL, rewrites any eid whose calendar segment is `primary` (or empty) to use the signed-in user's email. I verified the rebuilt eid is byte-for-byte identical to Google's.

I intentionally left `calendar-sync.ts` and the resolution path on `primary` — that's the correct, valid alias for the API, and only the web-editor URL needs the email form. The content-script button path was already correct (it carries the page's real `data-eventid`).

I now have the full picture. Here's the root cause and the fix.

## Root cause

The "Visitor event needs passes" banner has two sources:
- **Authoritative sync** (`calendar-sync.ts`) — correctly drops `cancelled`/deleted events (`:51,:63`).
- **Optimistic, DOM-derived nudge** (`calendar.content.ts:148-164`) — fires the instant a magic-address event surface *disappears*, on the assumption "saved & left."

**Deleting an event closes its surface exactly like saving-and-leaving does.** So the optimistic path fires for the just-deleted event. Worse, `setTargets` only clears an optimistic entry when a matching *real* target arrives from sync (`:600`) — a deleted event never will, so the stale nudge lingers and "Manage" opens the panel for a ghost event.

## Fix

Make the authoritative sync purge the optimistic guess: thread the set of `cancelled` event ids from sync → background → content, and drop them from the optimistic map. Plus a TTL safety-net so any unconfirmed optimistic guess self-expires instead of lingering forever.

Let me implement.

content.text:
Both pass. Here's what I changed and why.

## What was happening
Deleting a magic-address event fired the **optimistic, DOM-derived nudge** in the content script. That path (`calendar.content.ts:148-164`) shows the banner the instant an event's surface disappears, assuming "saved & left" — but **deletion closes the surface identically**, so it nudged for the ghost event. And since optimistic entries were only ever cleared when a matching *real* synced target arrived (which never happens for a deleted event), the stale nudge persisted and "Manage" opened the panel for it.

## The fix (sync is the source of truth)
1. **`calendar-sync.ts`** — `runSync` now collects `cancelledIds`: events seen this cycle that are `cancelled` (deleted) or dropped out of the marked set.
2. **`background.ts`** — `broadcastNudge` forwards those ids to calendar tabs alongside the marked targets.
3. **`calendar.content.ts`** — `setTargets(targets, cancelled)` **purges** any optimistic guess the sync resolved away, so a deleted event can't keep nudging or reopen the panel.
4. **TTL safety-net** — every optimistic guess now self-expires after 12s unless the sync promotes it to a real target. This catches the deletion case *and* any other unconfirmed guess (already-handled, magic address removed) instead of letting it linger forever.

I deliberately did **not** try to detect deletion from the DOM (snackbar text / grid chips) — that's i18n-fragile and risks suppressing legitimate nudges. Routing the truth through the sync is robust and self-correcting.

## One honest caveat
There's still a brief window: the banner can flash for a just-deleted event until the burst sync confirms the cancellation. The burst fires at 0/3/8s, but it only clears once **Google's events.list API** reflects the deletion (their eventual-consistency lag), then the purge removes it; the 12s TTL is the hard backstop. Eliminating the flash entirely would require DOM deletion-sniffing, which isn't worth the fragility. If that flash proves annoying in practice, the next lever is a short confirm-delay before the optimistic fires — tell me and I'll add it.

Needs a rebuild + extension reload + Calendar tab reload to take effect (same as before). Want me to run `/code-review` on this diff?

content.text:
Both pass. Here's what I changed.

## Behavior now
`findAnchor()` (in `calendar.content.ts`) places the **Manage Visitors** button *inside* the surface, with a graceful fallback chain:

1. **Event edit page** (`/eventedit`) → injected **just above the Description section** in the details tab. Fallback: before the native **Save** button (the old spot). 
2. **Detail popover / modal** → injected **right below the organiser line**, at the bottom. Fallback: appended to the very bottom of the dialog.
3. **Floating button** → only if neither surface offers an anchor (now a true last resort, not the default for popovers).

The injected button gets `block` styling (its own line, `margin:12px 0 4px`) for the new in-surface spots, and stays inline (`margin-right:8px`) only for the before-Save fallback.

## How it stays robust
- **Text/ARIA only, never CSS classes** — per the content-script contract. Description is found via `[aria-label*="description" i]`/placeholder; the organiser via the visible "Organiser/Organizer" label text; sections via a structural climb (`sectionAncestor`) to the form's section row.
- **Self-healing** — the existing `MutationObserver` re-runs `render()`, and `ensureInjected` re-injects if Google re-renders and drops our button. I also reordered `ensureInjected` to skip the anchor scan once the button is already placed (no per-tick popover scanning).
- Layered fallbacks mean a DOM change at Google's end degrades to a sensible spot rather than vanishing.

## One honest caveat
The exact placement depends on Google Calendar's current DOM, which I can't inspect from here. The Description `aria-label`, the literal "Organiser/Organizer" label, and the "section = parent with ≥3 children" heuristic are best-effort and English/ARIA-based. If the live result is off by a row, it's a one-selector tune — the structure is isolated in `findEditDescription` / `findOrganiserRow` / `sectionAncestor`.

Needs the usual rebuild + extension reload + Calendar tab reload. Want me to launch it and verify the placement visually (via the `verify`/`run` flow), or will you check it on your side first?

content.text:
Build and type-check pass. Here's the reworked injection.

## What changed

**No more bare-button overlap.** Instead of dropping a `<button>` into Google's section markup, I now inject a **full-width row** (`.auxilio-mv-row`, `display:flex; width:100%`) that flows as its own block — it can't overlap siblings. The button sits inside that row, indented via `contentInset()` to line up with the section's content column (measured as the gap past Google's leading icon gutter, clamped 0–120px).

**Placement matches your spec:**
- **Edit page** → inserted **between Description and the next section** (Visibility) via `section.nextSibling`, as its own row.
- **Modal/popover** → appended as the **last row of the content list** (the organiser line's row container), aligned under the organiser text above it.
- **Floating** → now a genuine last resort, only when no in-surface anchor exists.

**Native look.** The button is restyled as a Material **outlined** Calendar button via a one-time stylesheet: `Google Sans` 14/500, pill radius, `min-height:40px`, 1px `#747775` border, a proper hover/active **state-layer** and `:focus-visible` ring. It's brand-tinted (purple text/icon `#92288e`) rather than a solid purple fill, so it reads as part of Google's UI rather than an overlay.

**Interaction safety.** The button keeps its own `shieldInteractions`; the new row adds `shieldPadding`, which blocks taps on the row's *empty padding* (not the button) from bubbling to Google and dismissing the dialog. The `MutationObserver` still re-injects if Google re-renders.

## Honest caveat
I'm still inferring Google's DOM (the `aria-label*="description"` match, the literal "Organiser/Organizer" label, the "section = parent with ≥3 children" climb, and the inset measurement). These are isolated in `findEditDescription` / `findOrganiserLabel` / `sectionAncestor` / `contentInset`, so tuning is a one-spot change — but I can't confirm pixel placement without seeing it live.

Given we've now iterated on DOM specifics a few times, I'd suggest I **launch the extension and inspect the real Calendar DOM** so I can lock the selectors/alignment exactly instead of inferring. Want me to do that via the verify/run flow? It needs loading the unpacked build in Chrome and a Google login on your side.

content.text:
Done. Type-check and build pass.

## What changed

The placement bugs both came from picking the wrong container with a child-count heuristic. I replaced it with **geometry-based** detection that finds the real vertical content column and appends our row at its bottom.

**New helpers (in `calendar.content.ts`):**
- `stacksVertically(el)` — true when an element's visible children are stacked into a column (each child starts below the previous), not laid out side by side. This is what tells a vertical sections list apart from a horizontal `icon | text | value` row.
- `fullRowAncestor(el)` — climbs to the topmost ancestor that fills its parent's width *and* sits in a vertically-stacking parent. That's the section's own full-width row, so our button becomes its **own row, never inline**.
- `mainVerticalList(root)` — the largest vertically-stacking container in the dialog; its last child is the visual bottom.

**Placement now:**
- **Edit screen** → appended to the bottom of the **sections column** (`fullRowAncestor(description).parentElement`), so it's its own full-width row below the last section (Availability/Visibility), aligned under the field content via `contentInset`. No more inline-to-the-right.
- **Modal** → appended as the **last row of the content list**, after every element.

The native outlined-button styling, padding shield, and `MutationObserver` re-injection are unchanged.

## Caveat (same as before)
This is still inferring Google's DOM by geometry rather than seeing it. Geometry is more robust than the old child-count guess, but I can't confirm pixel placement from here. We've now iterated on this several times blind — I'd genuinely recommend letting me **launch the extension and inspect the live Calendar DOM** so I can lock it exactly in one pass instead of another round-trip. That needs the unpacked build loaded in Chrome with your Google login. Want to do that? Otherwise, reload and tell me what you see.

## What changed — now keyed off a stable, class-free signal

I dropped the brittle geometry guessing. Every native Calendar content row shares one structural trait that survives Google's class churn: **its first child is a decorative `aria-hidden` leading icon, followed by content** (confirmed in the DOM you pasted — `div.zZj8Pb[aria-hidden]` + `div.toUqff`). I now use that to find the rows list directly.

New helpers:
- **`looksLikeRow(el)`** — first child is an `aria-hidden` icon (or contains an `svg`/`img`/`i.google-material-icons`) with content after it.
- **`iconLedRowCount(container)`** — counts those rows, but only ones that span ≥50% of the container width, so a description **formatting toolbar** (lots of tiny icon buttons) can't be mistaken for the sections list.
- **`rowsListFor(el)`** — climbs from a field/label to the first ancestor whose parent holds ≥2 such rows, returning `{ list, row }` (the rows list + the specific row containing `el`).

Placement:
- **Edit mode** → `rowsListFor(description)` → `list.insertBefore(ourRow, descriptionRow)` = **above the Description section**.
- **Modal** → `rowsListFor(organizer)` → `list.appendChild(ourRow)` = **bottom of the content list** (after the last row), scrolling with the content.

Left indent still comes from `rowInset`/`firstTextLeft` (aligns to where the row's text actually paints), and the fallbacks (`scrollContainer`/`mainVerticalList`, before-Save inline, floating) remain.

Here's what I found and what I suggest.

**Current behavior:** `render()` hides the button whenever the panel is open (`button.update(false)`), and the click always sends `OPEN_FOR_EVENT`, which calls `chrome.sidePanel.open()`.

**The clean fix** leans on a distinction the codebase already has: `OPEN_FOR_EVENT` opens the panel + sets the active event, while `FOLLOW_EVENT` only points the already-open panel at an event (no `sidePanel.open`). So:

1. **Stop hiding** — always show the button on a surface; keep auto-follow when the panel's open.
2. **Sanity guard** — when the panel is already open, the click sends `FOLLOW_EVENT` instead of `OPEN_FOR_EVENT`, so it never fires a second open (and it usefully re-points the panel at whatever event you clicked).
3. **Race guard** — an `opening` latch covers the gap between the first click and the panel actually connecting, so two fast clicks can't both call `sidePanel.open`. It clears on `PANEL_STATE(true)` or a 3s timeout fallback. This stacks on top of `wireActivate`'s existing in-flight double-fire guard.

Type-check and build pass.

## What I changed

**1. Button stays visible when the panel is open.** `render()` now always does `button.update(!!surface)` regardless of `panelOpen`, and still calls `maybeFollow()` while open so the panel keeps tracking the event you're viewing. The button only disappears when there's no open event surface — as before.

**2. Sanity guard against duplicate opens.** The click handler now branches on panel state:
- **Panel closed** → `OPEN_FOR_EVENT` (the only path that calls `chrome.sidePanel.open`), guarded by an `opening` latch.
- **Panel open (or mid-open)** → `FOLLOW_EVENT`, which just re-points the existing panel at the clicked event. No second `sidePanel.open`.

**3. Race guard.** `opening` is set when the first open is dispatched and cleared either when the panel actually connects (`PANEL_STATE(true)`) or after a 3s timeout fallback (in case the open was blocked). So two rapid clicks before the panel finishes connecting can't both fire an open — the second sees `opening` and falls through to `FOLLOW_EVENT`. This layers on top of the existing `wireActivate` in-flight double-fire guard.

## Nice side effect
While the panel is open, the button now acts as an explicit "show this event in the panel" control — clicking a different event's button re-points the panel to it via `FOLLOW_EVENT`, which complements the existing auto-follow.

One thing I deliberately left alone: the **nudge banner** still hides while the panel is open (`nudge.setPanelOpen`). You only asked about the Manage Visitors button, and the nudge is a separate "you haven't acted yet" prompt where hiding-on-open still makes sense. Say the word if you want that shown too.

content.text:
This is a regression from the "keep the button visible when panel is open" change. Here's the chain: clicking a list item navigates the tab to the edit page; during load the panel is open and a surface is detected, but `findAnchor()` can't find the description/availability yet, so `ensureInjected()` fails and the **floating fallback** pops up — until the page finishes loading and the native button injects.

The right fix: **the floating fallback should never show while the panel is open.** The FAB's only job is to open/surface the panel when there's no clean anchor — but if the panel is already open, it's redundant (auto-follow already points it at the viewed event). content.text:
Build passes.

## What caused it & the fix

"Extension context invalidated" happens when the extension is **reloaded/updated** (constant in dev) while a Calendar tab is still open. The old content script keeps running but its `chrome.runtime` is dead, so its `setInterval`, `MutationObserver`, and follow/sync calls keep invoking `chrome.runtime.sendMessage` — which **throws synchronously**, and a `.catch()` can't catch a synchronous throw, so it surfaces as *uncaught*.

I made the content script orphan-safe:

1. **`extAlive()`** — `!!chrome.runtime?.id`, which becomes falsy the moment the context dies.
2. **`safeSend()` / `safeStorageGet()` / `safeStorageSet()`** — wrappers that check `extAlive()` and `try/catch` around the chrome call, so nothing can throw or reject regardless of context state. Every `chrome.runtime.sendMessage` and `chrome.storage.session.*` call in the content script (main flow, `openFor`/`followFor`, and the nudge banner) now goes through these.
3. **`teardown()`** — once an orphan is detected (guarded at the top of `render()` and inside the 1s `editorPoll`), it `clearInterval`s the poll and `observer.disconnect()`s the MutationObserver, so the dead script stops doing work entirely instead of firing safe-but-pointless calls forever.

Net effect: after an extension reload, the old script goes quiet immediately — no console error spam, no wasted polling/observing. The fresh content script that WXT injects on the next tab reload takes over.

Note: this is a defensive fix for an orphaned script; the underlying trigger is reloading the extension, so the existing open tab still needs a refresh to get the *new* code (only the error is silenced). The `chrome.runtime.onMessage.addListener` call I left as-is — registering a listener doesn't throw, and a dead context simply never delivers messages to it.

Reload the extension + Calendar tab and the uncaught error should be gone.

content.text:
Type-check and build pass.

## Why the reload happened

The click handler tries to take you to the event's edit screen, *unless you're already there*. But "already there" was tested with `!urlEid()` — i.e. "is there no event id in the URL?" A **brand-new, unsaved event has no id in its `/eventedit` URL**, so that check read as "not on the edit screen" and fired `NAVIGATE_TO_EVENT`. Background then did a `chrome.tabs.update(... url ...)` = a full tab navigation → the editor's `beforeunload` guard shows **"Leave site? Changes you made may not be saved"** → your in-progress event is discarded on reload. (The navigation target was a stale `clickedEid`, compounding it.)

## Fix

The guard now keys on whether you're on an `/eventedit` page at all, not on whether the URL has an id:

```js
const onEditPage = /\/eventedit/.test(location.pathname);
if (snap.eid && !onEditPage) { …navigate… }
```

So:
- **Creating/editing in the full editor** (new or existing) → never navigates → no reload, no "Leave site?", nothing lost. The side panel still opens via `openFor`/`followFor` (which use messaging + storage, not navigation).
- **From a detail popover** (not an `/eventedit` page) → still navigates you to the edit screen for that existing event, as before.

Reload the extension + Calendar tab and retry: create a new event, click Manage Visitors — the panel should open with no page reload and no leave-site prompt.

One adjacent thing I noticed but did **not** change: on the new-event editor the panel opens against whatever `snap.eid` is (often empty → it shows the "save the event to issue passes" notice, which is correct; but a stale `clickedEid` could in theory point it at the wrong event). If you ever see the panel open to the *wrong* event from a new-event editor, tell me and I'll tighten how `clickedEid` is cleared — it's a separate, smaller issue from the reload.

content.text:
Right — and this is the key nuance that makes skipping the navigation safe rather than lossy. Let me separate the two things, because "we have the right id" and "we can act on it" aren't the same here.

## The id itself: yes, it's the stable one
When you open the **full editor** for a new event, Google pre-allocates the event id and puts it in the `/r/eventedit/<eid>` URL. That decodes to `<eventId> <calendarId>` exactly like the saved ones we decoded earlier (`786oqbqr… junaid@appointy.com`), and Google keeps that same `eventId` when you hit Save — the "temporary" id *is* the eventual real id. Our `readSurface()` reads it via `urlEid()`, so the snapshot's `eid` for a new-event editor is that durable id, not a throwaway. So your instinct is correct: we're picking up the id that becomes the actual one.

## But we still can't resolve it until it's saved
Having the right id ≠ being able to issue passes. The engine resolves an event by its canonical **`iCalUID`** via the Calendar API (`events.get`). An unsaved event has **no server record and no `iCalUID` yet** — `events.get` would 404 — which is why the panel shows "Save the event to issue passes" for a new event. Once you save:
- the same id now exists on the server (with an `iCalUID`),
- the background sync + auto-follow (`FOLLOW_EVENT`) pick it up by that id, and the panel resolves normally.

So nothing is lost by *not* navigating: we're already on that event's editor, the panel is already pointed at it, and the moment you save, it becomes resolvable under the same id. Navigating would have re-loaded the tab (even to "the same" event, since the URL encoding differs — `primary` vs your email, padding, etc.) and discarded the unsaved form — all cost, zero benefit.

## One caveat worth flagging
This clean story holds **when the new-event URL actually carries the eid** (the full editor). If you start from an entry point where the URL has *no* eid (e.g. the small quick-create popover), `readSurface()` falls back to `clickedEid`, which could be stale from a previously viewed event — pointing the panel at the wrong event. In practice the `MutationObserver` usually clears `clickedEid` in that state (so `snap.eid` becomes `''` → "unsaved" notice, which is correct-ish), but it's not airtight.

If you'd like, I can harden that: on an `/eventedit` page with no `urlEid()`, force `snap.eid = ''` (treat as a genuinely-new unsaved event) instead of ever using a stale `clickedEid`. Want me to do that?

content.text:
Type-check and build pass.

## What was causing the intermittency

Injection only happened when `render()` ran (on clicks, popstate, or a `MutationObserver` childList change). But a surface's **layout can settle without a DOM mutation** — Google renders the rows, then a later layout pass gives them their width. Our anchor finders (`findContentList`, `firstIconLedRow`, `rowInset`) rely on `getBoundingClientRect().width`, so if `findAnchor` ran a beat too early (widths still 0, width-filter excludes the rows), it returned no anchor — and with no further childList mutation, `render()` never fired again, so we never retried. Whether the timing worked out was basically luck → "sometimes renders, sometimes doesn't."

## Fix

`update()` now runs a **bounded retry loop**: it tries to inject, and if the anchor isn't ready yet, it retries every 150ms for ~4.5s (30 attempts), stopping the instant injection succeeds. This guarantees we catch the moment Google finishes rendering/laying out the surface, independent of whether a mutation fires.

I also made the floating fallback show only after a **~450ms grace period** (`attempts >= 3`) rather than immediately — so on a normally-rendering surface the native in-surface button just appears, with no flash of the FAB getting swapped out a moment later. The FAB still appears as a genuine fallback if injection truly can't find an anchor.

`clearTimeout(retryTimer)` at the top of `update()` ensures overlapping calls (observer + retry) don't stack timers, and `update(false)` cancels any pending retry when the surface closes.

Reload the extension + Calendar tab and exercise it a few times — open/close the modal and the edit screen repeatedly; the button should now appear reliably each time rather than intermittently.

Type-check and build pass.

## Root cause

When I added the safe wrappers earlier, I converted every `chrome.*` call **except one**: `maybeFollow()` still called `chrome.runtime.sendMessage(...).catch(...)` directly. That function runs from `render()` whenever the panel is open and you're viewing a saved event — and `sendMessage` **throws synchronously** the moment the context is invalidated (an extension reload), which the `.catch()` can't intercept → uncaught error. The `render()` guard normally stops this, but there's a window where the context dies between the guard check and the `sendMessage` call (and the error context — `?msg=Could+not+find+the+requested+event` — is exactly the kind of navigation churn that races with a reload).

## Fix

`maybeFollow()` now uses `safeSend()` like every other call site. I verified with grep that the **only** remaining `chrome.runtime.sendMessage` / `chrome.storage.session.*` references are inside the three guarded wrappers (`safeSend`, `safeStorageGet`, `safeStorageSet`), plus `chrome.runtime.onMessage.addListener` (registration only — never throws) and `extAlive()`'s `chrome.runtime?.id` check (wrapped in try/catch). So there are no unguarded chrome API calls left in the content script.

content.text:
Good instinct — I'd push for it, and actually go a step further. Let me give you the reasoning, then implement it.

## My take: yes, remove the auto-navigation

The auto-nav is a **tab hijack with no functional payoff**:

1. **The panel doesn't need it.** The panel resolves the event itself via the Calendar API (`events.get` → `iCalUID`). The edit screen being open contributes nothing to issuing passes. As you say, there's no realtime calendar-edit sync that would justify it.
2. **It fights our constraints.** It's a full page reload — slow on mid/low-end Android and patchy warehouse Wi-Fi, exactly the conditions we optimize for. Spending a reload for zero function is the wrong trade.
3. **It's surprising.** The user clicked "Manage Visitors" expecting a panel, not for their whole calendar view to jump and reload. "Don't hijack the user" is one of our house rules.
4. **It's been a bug factory.** The new-event "Leave site?" data loss, the cross-serving `primary`-vs-email URL bug, the list-item-opening-edit-screen complaint — all downstream of auto-nav.

One refinement on your proposal: make it **one** explicit "Open in Calendar" control living in the panel's event summary (low-prominence, not a big CTA — registering visitors stays the primary action). Then *every* entry point (in-surface button, nudge, list pick) just opens the panel, and the single panel button is the one predictable way to jump to the event. That's more consistent than scattering nav across triggers.

So I'll: (1) drop the `NAVIGATE_TO_EVENT` call from the in-surface button, the nudge "Manage", and the panel's list-pick; (2) add an "Open in Calendar" button to the panel summary.

content.text:
Type-check and build pass.

## Fix

The problem was `paddingLeft: 0, paddingRight: 0` on the text button — that strips its 12px horizontal padding, so the touch target shrinks to the text bounds and the hover state-layer hugs the label, making it look like cramped raw text rather than a button.

I replaced it with `marginLeft: calc(-1 * var(--space-md))` (−12px = the text button's own padding). This is the standard MD3 text-button placement: the **label** lines up flush with the card's content edge, while the padding, hit area, and hover/press state layer all stay intact. So it now reads as a proper, tappable text button that's visually aligned with the title/metadata above it.

Reload and check the panel — the "Open in Calendar" button should look like an intentional secondary action (primary-colored label + external-link icon, proper hover) sitting just under the event details, aligned to the card edge.

If by "not properly designed" you meant the **placement** rather than the button styling (e.g. you'd prefer it as an icon action next to the title, or as a footer action at the bottom of the card), tell me which and I'll move it — that's a quick change.

content.text:
Type-check and build pass.

## What the bug is

Two things, and I hardened both:

**1. Debounce starvation during scroll (the likely live-context cause).** The `MutationObserver` that drives re-injection is debounced at 150ms. A continuous scroll makes Google fire DOM mutations *faster* than 150ms (lazy rendering, hovercards), so the debounce timer keeps resetting and `render()` never actually runs while you're scrolling. If Google re-renders the modal body and detaches our appended button in that window, there's nothing to put it back until you stop scrolling — so it "disappears for a few seconds."

**Fix:** the existing 1s `editorPoll` now also calls `render()` each tick. That's a debounce-independent safety net — even if the observer is starved, the button is re-asserted (re-injected if missing) within ~1s.

**2. Orphaned content script (if you reloaded the extension without reloading the tab).** Once the context is invalidated, `teardown()` disconnects the observer, so after Google removes the button on scroll it can never come back — and previously it left a *dead* button that looks clickable but does nothing.

**Fix:** `teardown()` now also removes the button (`button.update(false)`), so an orphaned script cleans up instead of leaving a zombie. The real remedy for this case is reloading the Calendar tab to get a fresh, live content script.

## So, which is it for you?
- If it comes back after you stop scrolling → that was the debounce starvation, now covered by the 1s re-assert.
- If the button just vanishes and never returns even when idle → that's the orphaned script; **reload the Calendar tab** after reloading the extension.

Reload the extension **and the Calendar tab**, then open a modal and scroll continuously — the button should now persist (or re-appear within ~1s) rather than dropping out.

If it still vanishes on a freshly-reloaded tab, the next best diagnostic is to paste the modal's scroll container DOM (the element that actually scrolls) — Google may be virtualizing/recycling the rows, in which case I'd anchor our button outside the recycled region instead of inside the rows list.
